recommendedNotes: Slice 1 cm thick. Steam until fork-tender; retains beta-carotene better than boiling.
recommendedReason: Steaming preserves beta-carotene and vitamin C while fully breaking down cell walls dogs can't digest raw.

sous_vide.notes: Slice 3–5 mm so the bag conforms. Use a dedicated 85 °C bath — vegetables will not soften at meat-cooking temperatures.

oven.notes: Cube 2 cm and toss in a little oil. Roasting concentrates beta-carotene more than steam.

slow_cooker.warning: Slow cookers turn carrots mushy — fine for a stew, less ideal as a standalone side.

pressure_cooker.notes: 1 cm chunks, high pressure with 1 cup water, quick release.

recommendedNotes: Cube 2–3 cm. Roast plain — no sugar, no spice. Mash before serving.
recommendedReason: Roasting drives off water and concentrates the soluble fiber that regulates stool — the main reason pumpkin is in the bowl.

stovetop_low.notes: Steam over a colander; boiling waterlogs it and dilutes the fiber benefit.

slow_cooker.warning: Will be very wet — drain or reduce liquid before mashing.

pressure_cooker.notes: 2 cm chunks, high pressure, natural release.

recommendedNotes: Always cooked — raw sweet potato is hard to digest. Cube or bake whole and mash.
recommendedReason: Roasting caramelizes starch into easily digestible sugars and is the most palatable preparation. Sous-vide produces a sweeter result thanks to slow enzymatic conversion, if you have the time.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: cap at 1 tablespoon per meal — high starch.

rawBlockedReason: Raw sweet potato contains trypsin inhibitors and is essentially indigestible.

sous_vide.notes: 1 cm cubes, dedicated 85 °C bath. Beta-amylase converts starch to maltose — comes out noticeably sweeter than baked.

stovetop_low.notes: Steam cubed; boiling raises the glycemic load.

pressure_cooker.notes: 2 cm chunks, high pressure, natural release.

recommendedNotes: Florets only, no thick stems. Keep al dente — overcooking destroys vitamin C.
recommendedReason: Short steaming keeps vitamin C and sulforaphane intact while softening the florets enough for canine digestion.

sous_vide.notes: Small florets, single layer in the bag. Sulfur smell will be intense on opening — this is normal.
sous_vide.warning: Florets go olive-drab in a sealed bag; nutritionally fine, just visually unappealing.

oven.warning: Some dogs refuse the Maillard browning on brassicas — try a small batch first.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure — the come-up cooks them. Quick-release immediately or they turn to mush.

recommendedNotes: Wilt briefly in boiling water, drain, chop. Reduces oxalates slightly.
recommendedReason: A brief boil-and-drain reduces oxalates that interfere with calcium absorption, while preserving folate and iron.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: avoid, or limit to a teaspoon weekly — oxalate load is a concern for stone-prone breeds (Burmese, Persian, Himalayan).

recommendedNotes: Trim ends. Steam until bright green and tender-crisp. Can be served raw too.
recommendedReason: Steaming softens the pod fiber without leaching vitamin K and manganese — the nutrients that make green beans a go-to filler for weight-loss diets.

sous_vide.notes: Trim ends, keep whole — they hold shape beautifully.

oven.warning: Tends to dry them out — pets usually prefer steamed.

recommendedNotes: Slice or cube. Safe raw — cooking only softens for picky eaters.
recommendedReason: Light steaming softens for picky eaters without destroying the high water content that helps hydration.

sous_vide.warning: Releases a lot of water into the bag — drain before serving.

slow_cooker.warning: Turns to mush — fine in a stew, not as a side.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure, quick release — overcooks instantly.

recommendedNotes: Fresh or frozen (thaw first). Never use canned (added sodium).
recommendedReason: Quick steam preserves lutein and B-vitamins that boiling leaches out.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure, quick release. Frozen peas only need to thaw.

recommendedNotes: Strip leaves from tough stems, chop fine. Sparingly — oxalates + thiocyanates.
recommendedReason: Brief steaming deactivates the goitrogenic enzymes that interfere with thyroid function while keeping iron and vitamin K available.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: avoid if on thyroid medication. Otherwise occasional only.

rawBlockedReason: Raw kale's enzymes activate goitrogenic thiocyanates and make oxalate more bioavailable.

sous_vide.warning: Texture goes slick in a sealed bag — most pets prefer it steamed.

oven.warning: This is effectively a kale-chip prep — treat-portion, not a side.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure — best method for reducing oxalates in kale, beets and spinach.

recommendedNotes: Peel and cube 2 cm. Cook fully — raw parsnips are very fibrous.
recommendedReason: Steam fully softens the dense fiber and breaks down furanocoumarins that irritate the gut when raw.
rawBlockedReason: Raw parsnips contain furanocoumarins (psoralens) — phototoxic and GI-irritating.

sous_vide.notes: 1–2 cm chunks. Below 85 °C they stay fibrous — use the higher 'firm root' bath temperature.

pressure_cooker.notes: 2 cm chunks, high pressure, natural release.

recommendedNotes: Trim greens, scrub. Cook whole then peel; expect colored stool — harmless.
recommendedReason: Steaming retains betalains and folate that boiling washes away — these are the entire reason beets are in the bowl.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: avoid — high oxalate load is a concern for stone-prone breeds.

sous_vide.notes: Whole at 2–3 hr (skin slips off after), or 2 cm cubes at 90 min. Best method for retaining betalains.

oven.notes: Wrap whole in foil with a splash of water. Concentrates flavor.

stovetop_low.warning: If you boil, include the cooking water in the bowl — that's where the betalain antioxidants ended up.

pressure_cooker.notes: Whole, high pressure, natural release. Pressure breaks down oxalates better than steaming.

recommendedNotes: Halve, scoop seeds. Roast cut-side down on parchment. Mash flesh.
recommendedReason: Roasting concentrates the beta-carotene and produces the sweetness that makes squash one of the most palatable veg additions.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: cap at 1 tablespoon per meal — high starch.

sous_vide.notes: 2 cm cubes, single layer. Comes out silky.

stovetop_low.notes: Steam cubed; boiling waterlogs the flesh.

slow_cooker.warning: Will be very wet — drain or reduce liquid before mashing.

pressure_cooker.notes: 2 cm chunks, high pressure, natural release.

recommendedNotes: Serve raw — high water content. Slice into sticks; cooking ruins texture.
recommendedReason: Cucumber is 95% water — cooking serves no purpose and destroys the hydration and crunch that are its only contribution.
cookingBlockedReason: Cooking turns cucumber into a translucent slime pets refuse — raw is the only sensible preparation.

recommendedNotes: Chop finely into 5 mm pieces to prevent choking. Strings are hard to digest.
recommendedReason: Raw celery preserves apigenin and its mild diuretic effect — just chop fine to avoid string-related choking.

speciesNotes.cat: Small cats and dogs under 10 kg: consider steamed instead — finer chop reduces choking risk.

sous_vide.notes: Softens the strings that otherwise pass through whole.

recommendedNotes: Trim woody ends. Steam until tender. Slice into bite-size pieces before serving.
recommendedReason: Steam softens the woody fibers while preserving folate — folate is asparagus's main nutritional contribution.

sous_vide.notes: Lay flat in the bag, single layer.

recommendedNotes: Halve sprouts, steam until tender. Limit portion size — known to cause gas.
recommendedReason: Steaming deactivates the thiocyanates that suppress thyroid function while keeping the texture pets actually eat.

speciesNotes.cat: Cats: avoid if on thyroid medication.

sous_vide.warning: Sulfur smell concentrates in a sealed bag — open with the lid pointed away.

oven.notes: Halve, cut-side down. Many dogs prefer this caramelized form to steamed.

pressure_cooker.notes: Low pressure, quick release. Longer and they turn to mush.
